title: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)
summary: The checked-in XML is a local corpus copy of University of Michigan item CME00108. U-M encoded only the bilingual text on printed pp. 210–224 of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article, omitting the article's pp. 207–209 introduction and reorganizing its page-by-page English/Latin parallel into separate continuous Middle English and Latin streams. Zupitza transcribed the incomplete Middle English Life of Saint Mary Magdalene from Durham University Library, Cosin MS V.ii.14, ff. 106r–111v, and printed beneath it the corresponding Latin from Theodor Grässe's 1890 edition of Jacobus de Voragine. Current Durham cataloging identifies the English text as the Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French Légende dorée; the exact French exemplar remains unidentified.
review_notes: The intellectual work cataloged in the parallel metadata manifest is the incomplete Middle English Gilte Legende chapter, not Zupitza's whole article, its German introduction, or Grässe's Latin comparison text.
Zupitza's legacy shelfmark 5.2.14 resolves to current Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14; it is not a separate Cathedral Library manuscript.

The current catalog HTML compresses the component extent to f.106r–v, but Zupitza's explicit f.106r–111v range, the manuscript's incomplete ending, and the digitized sequence establish the actual target extent as ff. 106r–111v.
The complete Durham digital object has 242 canvases from front cover through fore-edge. The work occupies canvases 221–232 and begins mid-page on f.106r after the preceding Life of Saint Margaret.
The last words on f.111v and in the English edition are 'anoyntid the bodi of hir with'; quire 14 lacks its eighth leaf after f.111, so the ending is physically lost.
The Latin stream in CME00108 is editorial comparison material from Grässe, not evidence that the cataloged medieval English work is intrinsically bilingual.
A representative surviving manuscript of Jean de Vignay's French tradition would not by itself be the Middle English translator's physical exemplar. No such exemplar is asserted here.
The ignored Durham bundle is a private research cache. Its CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license does not authorize adaptation or commercial reuse in a profit-enabled print-on-demand workflow.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00108
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO and VID all identify CME00108; the file is 55,403 bytes.
The 2016 TEMPHEAD records removal of a prose fragment at the beginning and structural review; this is an encoding-history note, not a medieval textual event.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00108
entities.description: A public digital transcription of Zupitza's English and Latin comparison text on printed pp. 210–224. It retains printed page breaks, manuscript folio milestones, numbered comparison units, superscript alignment letters, stars, notes, and italic typography, but separates the two printed parallel streams into consecutive DIV2 elements.

entities.label: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende)
alternate_labels: Here bigynnyth the lyfe of Marye Magdaleyn
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English prose chapter/version translated as part of the Gilte Legende, conventionally dated to about 1438. Cosin MS V.ii.14 preserves this witness from its rubric and opening through an incomplete final sentence; the abstract English work is distinct from the damaged physical witness and from Zupitza's edition.

date_statements.label: Translation
date_statements.value: c. 1438 as part of the Gilte Legende

notes: The Middle English translator is anonymous. Jacobus de Voragine and Jean de Vignay belong to source-work layers, not to the English work's direct authorship display.

entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena (1893)
entities.description: Zupitza's article gives a German codicological and textual introduction on pp. 207–210, then an incomplete Middle English transcription from Cosin MS V.ii.14 with Grässe's Latin text placed beneath it on pp. 210–224.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena in me. Prosa aus einer Handschrift der Kathedral-Bibliothek zu Durham,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 91, 47th year (Braunschweig: George Westermann, 1893), 207–224.
bibliographic.title: Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena in me. Prosa aus einer Handschrift der Kathedral-Bibliothek zu Durham
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen

entities.label: Durham University Library, Cosin MS V.ii.14
entities.description: A parchment codex written in England in the mid-fifteenth century. It contains Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, Benedict Burgh's Cato, and Middle English lives of Alexius, Margaret, and Mary Magdalene. The target Life begins after the Margaret text on f.106r and runs through f.111v, where the final sentence breaks because the last leaf of quire 14 is missing.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Mid-fifteenth century; Zupitza proposed approximately the third quarter of the fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: This dates the physical witness, not the c.1438 translation event.

holding.notes: Held in the Cosin Manuscripts at Durham University Library; the older Cathedral Library designation is retained only as a historical shelfmark.

physical_description.support: Parchment, with seventeenth-century paper among the preliminary leaves
physical_description.extent: [1] + 3 preliminary leaves + 111 folios + 2 stiff leaves + [1] leaf; foliated i–iii, 1–111
physical_description.format: Codex; one hand in textura quadrata, 32 long lines
notes: One quire is missing at the beginning; quire 14 wants its eighth leaf after f.111.
George Davenport dated his ownership inscription 1664; he gave the manuscript to Bishop Cosin's Library around 1670.
The relevant leaves use red rubrication, red and blue initials, and colored paraphs visible in the digitized images.
The codex was microfilmed in 1985/86 by the Hill Monastic Manuscript Library; copies are reported at HMML and Durham.

entities.label: Durham complete digital facsimile of Cosin MS V.ii.14
entities.description: The provider's complete 242-canvas color IIIF object, beginning with the front cover and ending with the fore-edge. The target work occupies canvases 221–232 (ff.106r–111v).
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with 242 color canvases

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 91 (1893)
entities.description: A 500-page complete-volume PDF. Zupitza's article appears at printed pp.207–224 / PDF pages and Internet Archive leaves 221–238; CME00108 begins with the text portion at printed p.210 / leaf n224.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF with OCR and item metadata

entities.label: Jean de Vignay, Vie de sainte Marie Madeleine in the Légende dorée
entities.description: The Middle French prose version of Jacobus de Voragine's legend translated by Jean de Vignay. Current Durham cataloging identifies this version as the source of the Gilte Legende chapter, but no surviving French codex is identified as the English translator's physical exemplar.

entities.label: Jacobus de Voragine, De sancta Maria Magdalena (Legenda aurea)
entities.description: Chapter 96 of the Latin Legenda aurea, the ultimate source work behind Jean de Vignay's French version and the Middle English Gilte Legende chapter. This abstract work is not equated with Grässe's particular printed text.

entities.label: Theodor Grässe, Legenda aurea, third edition (1890)
entities.description: The third edition of Jacobus de Voragine's Latin Legenda aurea explicitly named by Zupitza as his comparison text. Chapter 96 begins on printed p.407; Zupitza uses the portion corresponding to printed pp.408–415 and omits the opening name etymology.

bibliographic.citation: Jacobus de Voragine, Legenda aurea vulgo Historia Lombardica dicta, ed. Theodor Grässe, 3rd ed. (Vratislaviae: Apud Guilelmum Koebner, 1890).
bibliographic.title: Legenda aurea vulgo Historia Lombardica dicta

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Grässe's 1890 Legenda aurea
entities.description: A complete 987-page scan with OCR and item metadata. The exact Latin comparison passage used by Zupitza is at printed pp.408–415 / Internet Archive leaves n429–n436.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-edition PDF with OCR and item metadata

entities.label: Durham Archives and Special Collections catalog record for Cosin MS V.ii.14
entities.description: The current official manuscript description used for shelfmark, holding, contents, date, physical structure, provenance, digitization, microfilm history, and the modern source identification of the Mary Magdalene component.

scope.notes: Identifiers, structure, and text establish the local artifact as the repository copy of U-M item CME00108.

subject.description: The complete U-M item, organized as one Middle English and one Latin version stream.

object.description: The bilingual English/Latin text, excluding Zupitza's German introduction.

scope.notes: The source edition interleaves English and Latin on each printed page; the encoding serializes the English stream first and the Latin stream second.

object.description: The complete surviving manuscript portion of the Life of Saint Mary Magdalene.

assertion.notes: Zupitza explicitly names the manuscript and folios; the digitized incipit and incomplete final line independently match his transcription.

subject.description: The Latin comparison printed beneath the Middle English text.

object.description: The corresponding part of Grässe's chapter 96, with the opening name etymology omitted.
The abstract Middle English Gilte Legende chapter/version.

subject.description: The anonymous Middle English Gilte Legende chapter.

object.description: Jean de Vignay's Middle French version.

scope.notes: The lineage schema uses version_of for this translation relation. It does not identify the translator's physical French exemplar.

subject.description: Jean de Vignay's French translation of the Mary Magdalene chapter.

object.description: Jacobus de Voragine's Latin source chapter.

scope.notes: The relation is textual, not an assertion that a particular surviving Latin manuscript was used.

object.description: The abstract Latin Mary Magdalene chapter.

scope.notes: The target work is separately mapped to ff.106r–111v / canvases 221–232.

work_portion.label: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene
notes: The work starts mid-page on f.106r after the preceding Life of Saint Margaret.
The work ends mid-sentence at the foot of f.111v because the following leaf is missing.
The bundle retains the complete provider object, including covers, endleaves, blanks, adjacent works, stiff leaves, and edges.

The same complete-codex bytes are reused by hard link for another work in this manuscript; the archive's internal top-level folder name reflects the first cache consumer and does not change its source identity.
Image requests used the provider's IIIF service and a 1200-pixel width request, but the level-1 service capped delivered images at approximately 1024 pixels on the long edge.
Public access does not remove the CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 restrictions recorded below.

rights.component: Modern XML markup and repository-specific contribution
rights.basis: No file-specific license for the checked-in XML was established during this research. Public-domain status of the medieval text and 1893 edition does not automatically determine rights in modern markup.

rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan CME collection
rights.basis: The current U-M CME collection states that its materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright–United States.
notes: This provider assertion does not determine Durham manuscript-image rights or the local repository XML license.

rights.component: Medieval Middle English linguistic and literary content
rights.basis: The anonymous c.1438 translation and mid-fifteenth-century witness are centuries outside copyright term; this does not grant rights in modern photographs, editions, or markup.
The thirteenth-century Latin work is centuries outside copyright term; modern editions and images remain separately scoped.
Jean de Vignay's medieval French translation is centuries outside copyright term; modern editions and manuscript photographs remain separately scoped.

rights.component: 1893 article text, transcription, editorial comparison, and typography
rights.basis: The article was published in 1893 and is in the public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and access layer
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States and cites the scanned volume's 1893 date; no item-specific open-content license was captured for the scan files.

rights.component: Durham manuscript photographs and IIIF image delivery
rights.jurisdiction: Provider-stated license; worldwide reuse subject to applicable law
rights.basis: The IIIF Presentation manifest publishes the legal-code URI for CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 and attributes the digital object to Durham University Library.
notes: The license permits sharing only with attribution, for noncommercial purposes, and without adaptations. Separate written permission is needed before committing the facsimile to a commercial or profit-enabled repository, incorporating it in print-on-demand products, or publishing adapted crops or annotations.
Private research use to inspect layout, abbreviation signs, rubrication, and editorial treatment is distinct from redistribution of the photographs.
The cached bundle remains Git-ignored and is not itself proposed for publication.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1890 and is in the public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and access layer
rights.basis: Internet Archive item metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States; no item-specific open-content license was captured for the scan files.

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza explicitly says that he regulated the use of capital letters in the transcription.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English text on printed pp.210–224
editorial_practices.representation: Editorially regularized print capitalization retained by CME00108

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza treated a semicircular mark over certain final n letters as ornamental and omitted it, while listing every occurrence; he separately notes smaller flourishes over u in two words.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English transcription, especially final n forms
editorial_practices.representation: Omitted from the running transcription and documented in the German introduction

editorial_practices.description: Italic segments within Middle English words frequently represent expanded abbreviation signs or editorially supplied letters. Comparison with the Durham images confirms this use in representative forms, but italics elsewhere in the bilingual article also carry editorial commentary and corrections, so every HI segment must be interpreted in context.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English transcription on pp.210–224; 191 HI elements in the English XML stream
editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript abbreviation signs, omitted letters, damaged or uncertain readings, and editorial supplies
editorial_practices.representation: Italic type in Zupitza, retained as HI REND=italic in CME00108
notes: Do not globally reinterpret every italic span as one uniform medieval feature; German note text and bracketed Latin corrections also use italics.

editorial_practices.description: The manuscript opens the work with a red rubric and decorated initial, while the edition and XML preserve the words as an ordinary heading rather than reproducing the original color and spatial layout.
editorial_practices.source_feature: Red rubric, red and blue initial, and colored paraphs
editorial_practices.representation: Black print heading, encoded as HEAD

editorial_practices.description: Zupitza prints Grässe's Latin beneath the English. A star in the English marks an English addition, a star in the Latin marks an English omission, and matching superscript letters identify expressions that do not correspond exactly; he then discusses major deviations and translation errors.
editorial_practices.scope: The bilingual comparison on printed pp.210–224, numbered 1–107
editorial_practices.source_feature: Differences between the incomplete Middle English translation and Grässe's Latin text
editorial_practices.representation: Parallel print streams with stars, superscript letters, numbered units, and German notes; the markers are retained by CME00108

editorial_practices.description: The encoding omits Zupitza's German introduction on pp.207–210 and selects the English/Latin text beginning on p.210. It converts the edition's page-by-page vertical parallel into two consecutive DIV2 version streams, English first and Latin second.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp.210–224 / the complete encoded TEXT
editorial_practices.source_feature: A bilingual comparison interleaved on each printed page
editorial_practices.representation: One DIV2 LANG=enm followed by one DIV2 LANG=lat; page, folio, section, note, star, superscript-letter, and italic signals retained
notes: The resulting XML order is a digital editorial reorganization, not the physical layout of the 1893 pages.

evidence.summary: Identifies CME00108 and preserves the selected Zupitza text as consecutive Middle English and Latin version divisions, including printed page breaks, manuscript folio milestones, numbered comparison units, superscript letters, stars, footnotes, and italic spans.

evidence.locator: ETS/TEXT element counts and division boundaries
evidence.summary: Counts 1 DIV1, 2 DIV2, 1 HEAD, 2 P, 30 PB, 232 MILESTONE, 22 NOTE, 198 HI, and 368 SUP elements. The English stream has 118 milestones, 22 notes, 191 italic spans, and 14 descendant PB elements plus the shared opening p.210 PB; the Latin stream has 114 milestones and 15 PB elements.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'Das leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena / [ed. Julius Zupitza],' Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00108.
evidence.locator: Bibliographic header and complete full-text view
evidence.summary: Supports the stable item identity, title, editor, 1893 Braunschweig publication data, and public full-text access; the displayed content begins at printed p.210 with the Middle English heading and later supplies the Latin stream.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection information and rights statement.
evidence.locator: Collection description and Access and Use statement
evidence.summary: Supports the collection's educational and research access terms and its public-domain / No Copyright–United States assertion for distributed materials.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena in me. Prosa aus einer Handschrift der Kathedral-Bibliothek zu Durham,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 91 (1893): 207–224.
evidence.locator: Printed pp.207–224 / PDF pages and IA leaves 221–238; encoded selection pp.210–224
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the article's bibliographic identity; legacy shelfmark and ff.106r–111v; manuscript date and Davenport provenance; loss of the ending; Latin and proposed French source layers; Grässe edition used; comparison symbols; translation errors; East Midland linguistic characterization; capitalization normalization; omitted final-n flourishes; and the full English/Latin text.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for Archiv volume 91, item archivfrdasstu91brauuoft.
evidence.summary: Supports exact public PDF and OCR file names, file sizes, volume identity, and the provider's NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT / US item assertion. The generic metadata date 1846 is rejected for this volume because its scanned title page states 1893.

evidence.citation: Durham University Library Archives and Special Collections, Catalogue of Cosin MS V.ii.14.
evidence.locator: Manuscript introduction, physical description, item 5, microfilm, digitized material, and bibliography
evidence.summary: Current authority for shelfmark, holding, mid-fifteenth-century English production, physical structure, missing leaf after f.111, one hand, Davenport/Cosin provenance, target incipit and incomplete explicit, Middle English language, Jean de Vignay/Jacobus source chain, Zupitza edition, HMML microfilm, and October 2023 digitization.
notes: The HTML component-range display collapses to f.106r–v; the page's own incomplete explicit and missing-leaf description, Zupitza's range, and IIIF canvases establish ff.106r–111v.

evidence.citation: Durham University Library, IIIF Presentation manifest for Cosin MS V.ii.14, ARK ark:/32150/t2mjm214p80v.
evidence.locator: 242 canvases; target canvases 221–232; license and attribution
evidence.summary: Supports complete-object coverage from front cover through fore-edge, exact f.106r–111v canvas mapping, Durham attribution, image-service URLs, and the provider's CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license URI.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the complete Durham IIIF bundle for Cosin MS V.ii.14.
evidence.summary: Confirms that the target starts mid-page on f.106r with a red rubric and decorated initial, continues through twelve canvases, and ends at the foot of f.111v in the incomplete phrase represented by Zupitza and the XML. Representative comparisons also show that italicized letter groups in the edition can correspond to manuscript abbreviation signs.

evidence.citation: University of St Andrews, Middle English Texts Series, 'Three Lives from the Gilte Legende.'
evidence.summary: Describes the anonymous Gilte Legende as a major prose translation composed about 1438 and identifies the modern three-volume Early English Text Society edition by Richard Hamer and Vida Russell.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 197393, Gilte Legende.
evidence.summary: States that the Middle English Gilte Legende is translated from Middle French from Latin and closely follows, with additions and omissions, Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée, itself a close translation of Jacobus de Voragine's Legenda aurea.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Catalogue général record FRBNF31606933, Légende dorée / Jacques de Voragine; traduction de Jean de Vignay.
evidence.summary: Authoritative bibliographic evidence distinguishing Jacobus de Voragine as author of the Latin work from Jean de Vignay as translator of the French Légende dorée.

evidence.citation: Jacobus de Voragine, Legenda aurea vulgo Historia Lombardica dicta, ed. Theodor Grässe, 3rd ed. (Vratislaviae: Guilelmus Koebner, 1890), chapter 96.
evidence.locator: Title page; chapter 96, printed pp.407–416; comparison portion pp.408–415 / IA leaves n429–n436
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the exact third edition cited by Zupitza, its 1890 Vratislaviae imprint, chapter title and boundaries, and the Latin comparison passage selected beneath the English transcription.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for Jacobus de Voragine / Grässe, Legenda aurea (1890), item jacobiavoragine00jacogoog.
evidence.summary: Supports exact public PDF and OCR file names, sizes, 1890 imprint metadata, and the provider's NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT / US item assertion.

open_questions.question: Which French manuscript or textual family of Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée supplied the exemplar for the c.1438 Middle English Gilte Legende translation represented here?
next_steps: Consult Hamer and Russell's three-volume EETS Gilte Legende edition, especially its source and manuscript discussions.
Collate the Durham chapter's distinctive errors and additions against critical work on Jean de Vignay rather than selecting a convenient surviving French facsimile as the exemplar.
Retain the French relation at work level until a physical exemplar is evidenced.

open_questions.question: Can every italic segment in Zupitza's Middle English transcription be classified exactly as an abbreviation expansion, supplied letter, damaged reading, or another editorial function?
next_steps: Audit all 191 English HI spans against ff.106r–111v at the highest image resolution permitted by Durham.
Record segment-level categories without treating German note italics or bracketed Latin corrections as the same phenomenon.
Preserve the existing italic signal until a fully evidenced normalization can distinguish expansion from supply and commentary.

open_questions.question: What coverage, resolution, access procedure, and reuse terms apply to the 1985/86 HMML microfilm copies held by HMML and Durham?
next_steps: Ask Durham through its current contact form and search HMML Reading Room records by current and historical shelfmark.
Keep any supplied microfilm separate from the public color IIIF object and record its exact contractual terms.
